problem_summary: 2025 MCM Problem B (Sustainable Tourism) — design a tourism management model for Juneau, Alaska, a cruise-driven overtourism town, that balances economic gain, resident social satisfaction, and environmental/glacier protection; then extend it to another overtourism city and write a one-page memo to the Juneau Tourism Council. This O-award paper (Team 2502617) frames the whole problem as a single-decision-variable (number of tourists N_t) multi-objective optimization, monetizes all three benefit dimensions into USD, and solves for a Pareto-optimal tourist volume of ~1.32 million.
models_used: Multi-objective optimization (weighted-sum scalarization) over a single decision variable N_t = annual tourist count, with objectives max E (economic), max S (social), min Env (environmental)

Sub-model I — Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) with marginal effects: revenue C_Gi = alpha*N_t*t with a sigmoid/logistic decay alpha(N_t) capturing diminishing marginal returns, and convex (quadratic) increasing-marginal cost C_Gc = C_i*N_t^2 + C_fix; break-even points define feasible tourist range

Sub-model II — Social Welfare model: dimensionless satisfaction s = P_l * P_f built from living-pressure ratio (C_Ri/C_Rc) and congestion ratio (N_r/N_t), then monetized via logistic equivalent C_add = A_1 / (1 + e^((N_t - k_1*N_r)*t)); social benefit S = s * C_add

Sub-model III — Environmental Carrying Capacity / Carbon Footprint model: per-attraction carbon emissions E_ci = gamma*(r_i*N_t)^2 summed over attractions (penalizes tourist concentration via quadratic), monetized as C_CO2 = C_perC * E_CO2; government green spend C_env = N_t * A_2*t/(1+e^(k_2*t)); Env = C_CO2 - C_env

Mendenhall Glacier recession model (logarithmic): ΔV/V0 = lambda*ln(1 + ΔE_CO2/C_0) with climate sensitivity lambda=0.8°C/(W/m^2) — used only for the memo's 25-year/90-year glacier-loss forecast, deliberately NOT fed into the optimizer to avoid over-parameterization

Weighted-sum scalarization max T = w1*E + w2*S - w3*Env with weights derived from inverse state rankings (US state GDP, MERIC cost-of-living, Yale EPI): w_i = rk_i / sum(rk_i) giving Juneau w ≈ {0.346, 0.338, 0.316}
SLSQP (Sequential Least Squares Quadratic Programming) as the constrained nonlinear solver for the Pareto-optimal N_t under inequality constraints (E>0, S>S_min, Env<0, T>0, 0<N_t<N_tmax=1.67M)
key_techniques: Monetization of ALL three dimensions into a common USD unit — social satisfaction and carbon are converted to dollar equivalents via 'shadow pricing' (carbon trading rates, satisfaction value equivalents), giving dimensional consistency so the three objectives can be summed

Using logistic/sigmoid functions to encode diminishing marginal returns (revenue saturation) and convex quadratics to encode increasing marginal cost and concentration penalties — a clean way to bake economic 'laws' into closed-form objective functions
Break-even analysis to derive an interpretable feasible interval of N_t instead of arbitrary bounds (C_Gi = C_Gc gives upper/lower tourist thresholds)

Objective weights grounded in external authoritative rankings (state GDP/MERIC/Yale EPI) rather than guessed — the inverse-rank rule (worse rank => higher improvement need => higher weight) gives a defensible, transparent weighting story
Deliberate anti-over-parameterization discipline: the glacier model is computed for the narrative but excluded from the optimizer; carbon is used as the single environmental proxy

Spatial diversion lever via r_i (tourist share per attraction): the quadratic carbon term means total benefit is maximized when r_i ≈ 1/n (even dispersion), turning a math property into a concrete policy ('promote niche attractions')

Model transfer by parameter re-weighting: extension to Jiuzhaigou done by changing tax t=13% (China VAT) and weights w={0.625,0.313,0.062} to reflect economy-over-environment priorities — same structure, new constants

Sensitivity analysis on the three real policy levers (tourist proportion r, tax rate t, per-tourist consumption A_0) tied directly to the price-elasticity narrative (e.g., the $4000 inflection where tourism flips from inelastic to elastic)
why_it_won: Judge perspective: the paper nails the single most important thing in Problem B — it reduces a sprawling, fuzzy sustainability question to ONE interpretable decision variable (N_t) and a single comparable objective (everything in USD), which makes the trade-offs legible and the recommendation actionable ('cap tourists near 1.32M'). The economic story is sophisticated but disciplined: diminishing-marginal-return sigmoids and increasing-marginal-cost quadratics are textbook-correct and match the cost-benefit curves judges expect, and the break-even framing yields concrete tourist thresholds rather than hand-waving. It explicitly addresses all three required factors (carrying capacity, hidden costs, resident satisfaction) and monetizes the two hard-to-quantify ones, which directly answers the prompt. The weighting is defensible (external rankings, not arbitrary), the extension to Jiuzhaigou is genuinely structural (re-weighting + re-parameterizing, with a clean comparison table showing it captures real city differences), and the memo is specific and data-backed (25-year glacier-loss forecast, three named measures, an optimal tax of 0.58). It also shows self-awareness — a candid Strengths/Weaknesses section and an explicit choice to exclude the glacier model from the optimizer to avoid over-parameterization, which signals modeling maturity to judges.
section_highlights: Strong structure throughout: (1) Three parallel, symmetric sub-models (Economic / Social / Environmental), each with the same internal rhythm — benefit analysis, cost analysis, overall performance — making the paper easy to navigate and reinforcing the 'triangle' theme. (2) Every sub-model is paired with an interpretive figure (cost-benefit curve, satisfaction curve, environmental curve) plus a constraint heatmap, so each abstract formula is immediately visualized. (3) The integration section (4.4) cleanly composes the three sub-models into the weighted objective and constraints, with the weight-derivation method spelled out. (4) Excellent 'narrative spine': the title ('Overload Alarm: Pareto Optimization of the Economic-Social-Environmental Triangle') and the recurring triangle motif tie the whole paper together. (5) Concrete numerical payoff stated early in the Summary (1.32M tourists, $576M net income) and echoed in results and memo. (6) The Juneau-vs-Jiuzhaigou comparison Table 3 is a standout — it crisply separates 'similar parts' from 'different parts' and maps each difference to a parameter/weight change, demonstrating generalizability concretely rather than by assertion. (7) Clear Assumptions-with-Justifications section grounding each simplification in economics theory (convex/concave curves, shadow pricing, full-information market, partial equilibrium).
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy reliance on monetization conversion factors (C_add satisfaction-to-USD, C_perC carbon-to-USD) whose values are weakly sourced ('Estimate', 'Estimate from Article') — the headline result is sensitive to numbers that are essentially assumed

Several key elasticity parameters k_0=0.0068, k_1=0.3574, k_2=1.6 are labeled 'Estimate' with no fitting procedure or data shown — the logistic/sigmoid forms are plausible but their calibration is opaque and partly reverse-engineered to produce sensible curves

Internal numerical inconsistency / sloppiness: the Summary says weighted total benefit is '10 million USD', the results section says '8 million' and later '15 million' for total performance, and the memo's optimal tax (0.58) differs sharply from the model's baseline t=5% — these are not reconciled

Many embedded figures appear to be schematic/illustrative rather than actual solver output (round-number tables, smooth idealized curves), and some chart data tables look auto-extracted and garbled — the empirical grounding of the plots is questionable

Single decision variable (N_t) is a strong simplification: tax rate, prices, and dispersion r_i are treated as parameters/levers in sensitivity analysis rather than jointly optimized, so the 'global Pareto optimum' is really a 1-D scan plus SLSQP, not a true multi-variable Pareto frontier
Static model — explicitly ignores time lags, seasonality, external competition, and infrastructure decay (acknowledged in Weaknesses), so projections like the 1.32M optimum may not hold dynamically

The weighted-sum scalarization only finds Pareto points on the convex hull; the paper claims Pareto optimality but a weighted sum cannot recover non-convex regions of the frontier, a subtlety not addressed

Glacier recession model uses climate-sensitivity lambda in °C/(W/m^2) inside a volume-ratio log formula without a clear unit bridge from CO2 emissions to radiative forcing — dimensionally hand-wavy, though wisely kept out of the optimizer
reusable_patterns: Collapse a multi-stakeholder/multi-objective problem onto ONE interpretable decision variable, then make all objectives comparable by monetizing into a common unit — maximally legible and decision-ready

Encode economic 'laws' directly in functional forms: logistic/sigmoid for diminishing returns and saturation, convex quadratics for increasing marginal cost and for penalizing concentration (the (r_i*N_t)^2 dispersion trick is reusable for any 'spread vs. concentrate' problem)
Derive feasible bounds from break-even analysis (revenue=cost) instead of inventing arbitrary constraint limits

Justify objective weights with external authoritative rankings via an inverse-rank rule (w_i = rk_i/sum rk_i) — a defensible, citable alternative to AHP/entropy when you need a quick transparent weighting
Three-symmetric-sub-model architecture (each: benefit, cost, overall) for any economic-social-environmental / triple-bottom-line prompt — easy to write, easy for judges to follow
Pair every model with both an interpretive curve AND a constraint heatmap so formulas are always visualized

Handle the 'extend to another city' sub-question by KEEPING the model structure and only changing parameters + weights, then presenting a similar/different comparison table — strong, concrete evidence of generalizability

Explicitly exclude a tempting-but-noisy sub-model (here the glacier equation) from the optimizer to avoid over-parameterization, while still using it for narrative/memo impact — a maturity signal worth copying
Use SLSQP (scipy.optimize.minimize, method='SLSQP') as the go-to solver for smooth constrained nonlinear single/low-dimensional objectives
Make the memo concrete: name 2-3 levers, give a numeric target for each (optimal tax, optimal N_t, dispersion ~1/n), and a vivid stakes statement (glacier gone in 25/90 years)
